sources.notes: Chord-prefix task asking whether every chord appears an even number of times across all chains.
Good Bye 2025 editorial hints mention Fenwick tree of bitsets and XOR hashing.
Full-solution notes explain maintaining chain parity sets via XOR hashing.

annotation.summary: For each prefix of non-sharing chords on a circle, determine whether all chains make every chord appear even times.
annotation.constraints: Total endpoints up to 1e6, so explicit bitsets over all chains are too large.
annotation.core_idea: For a new chord i, the parity set of chords appearing in chains ending at i is the XOR of parity sets of previous intersecting chords plus itself. Chord intersection is an interval-crossing query on endpoints. Instead of storing full sets, assign random/hash values to chords and maintain XOR hashes with a Fenwick tree; a prefix is tight exactly when the accumulated parity hash is zero with high probability.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) with Fenwick-tree range XOR updates/queries.
tricks: reduce even-occurrence condition to parity sets
circle chord intersection as interval query

solution_variants.name: xor-hashed-parity-dp
solution_variants.summary: DP over chains ending at each chord, storing parity sets by XOR hash and querying intersecting previous chords.

tags.evidence: The recurrence computes parity information for chains ending at each newly added chord.
Full parity sets are represented by randomized/hash XOR values.
The core memory reduction is replacing large parity bitsets by XOR hashes.
tags.description: Represent a set or parity vector by XORing random/hash values assigned to elements.
tags.created_reason: XOR hashing is a specific randomized compression trick useful for retrieval.

tags.evidence: Fenwick tree supports interval/range XOR maintenance over chord endpoints.
Hash equality is probabilistic unless implemented with deterministic large bitsets.
